ItemGenerationAndUsage.custom:
## Rarity Tiers

Every equippable item (Armor, Weapon, Tool with a slot) carries a rarity tier. The tier is exposed in the description as a leading bracket: `[Common]`, `[Uncommon]`, `[Rare]`, `[Epic]`, `[Legendary]`. Consumables, currency, and readable items omit the tier bracket.

What each tier means in the world:

- **Common** — apprentice-grade; mass-produced or smith-bought; what a fresh practitioner carries. Single small bonus.
- **Uncommon** — journeyman-grade; properly fitted, institutional standard issue; what most working professionals carry. Moderate bonuses.
- **Rare** — senior-grade; hand-fitted, marker of standing within a profession or order. Strong bonuses.
- **Epic** — master-grade; few exist outside major institutional centers; carried by ranking masters or recovered from rich finds. Excellent bonuses.
- **Legendary** — pre-Ensoulment relic or unique commission; each one named, each with a story. Top bonuses, often four bonus types stacked.

Magnitude scaling by tier (typical, not rigid):

- Common: one bonus, value 1
- Uncommon: one or two bonuses, values 1–3
- Rare: two or three bonuses, values 2–5
- Epic: three or four bonuses, values 3–8
- Legendary: four bonuses, values 5–15

Values are not percentages. Engine bonuses are flat numeric modifiers. Higher than 15 even at Legendary should be exceptional.

## Slots

The world uses these equipment slots:

- `head`, `body`, `legs`, `feet`, `hands` — Armor (one each)
- `mainHand`, `offHand` — Weapon (one each)
- `trinket` — Tool (two; worn accessories like phial-bandoliers, source-binders, charms, relics)

Two-handed weapons (bows, polearms, two-handed swords) occupy `mainHand` only; their description must state the off-hand cannot hold anything else while the weapon is wielded. Most one-handed weapons leave `offHand` free for shields, focuses, or kits.

## Profession-Locking via Description

The schema does not enforce profession requirements on items. The AI narrator does. When generating a profession-locked item, state the lock in the description:

- **Wizard line items** (staves, focus-rings, mana-augmenting trinkets): "only wizards can work staff-work through it" or "a non-wizard can carry it but cannot draw on its mana-augmenting properties." Wizard professions: Wizard, Witch-Killer, Hedge-Wizard, Consort of the College.
- **Witch line items** (phial-bandoliers, source-binders, reservoir-augmenting trinkets): "a witch wearing this can fill more efficiently" or "the phials can only be drawn by someone with a reservoir." Witch professions: Witch, Hedge-Witch.
- **Prime line items** (ancestor-bone relics, ancestor-tokens): "only a Prime ancestor-mage can speak through it." Prime professions: Prime Ancestor-Mage, Prime Warden.
- **Generic items** (blades, bows, leather armor, plate, cloth robes, tools): no profession lock; anyone competent can wield them.

Non-magical professions (Soldier, Mercenary, Hunter, Mana-Hunter, Hold-Warrior, Crafter, Apothecary, Healer, Drifter) use generic items.

## Where Items Come From

Four main sources, each with its own flavor and access pattern:

### Institutional Issue

Brotherhood chapterhouses (staves for wizards, training to master tier), Queen's College quartermasters (phial-bandoliers and reagent kits for witches and consorts), kingdom smithies (blades, bows, plate for soldiers), Hofnar trade-wagons (everything portable; brokers between factions). Cost: paid in coin (gold/silver/copper). Higher tiers gated by rank or reputation with the institution. Brotherhood quartermasters do not issue master staves to journeymen; College does not issue master harnesses without the contract rank.

### Ruin and Quest Loot

Pre-Ensoulment vaults, family vaults, dwarf-style Vorok galleries, Wartide-overrun back-country sites. Found, not bought. Each piece has a story — who made it, who lost it, what it was for. Pre-Ensoulment items always require an apothecary or alchemist to identify properly; a merchant who skips the reading gets the price wrong.

### Curated City Shops

Each major city has its specialty:

- **Avenor** — phial-bandoliers, source-binders, College-licensed mana potions, witch reagents, consort contracts (which function like trinkets in the gear sheet)
- **Telinor (Kelmar)** — Brotherhood staves, wizard reagents, Brotherhood-stamped armor and blades, suppression tools used by Witch-Killers
- **Kelmar** broader market — soldier kit, march-line gear, Threshi-line specialty weapons
- **Udorath** — kingdom-issue heavy armor, agricultural tools repurposed for caravan defense, anti-Avenor political memorabilia and intelligence (sold as 'curiosities')
- **Nadorim** — coastal gear, fishing implements, light leathers, salt-cured rations, Quelled-trade contraband under the counter
- **Hofnar wagon-cities** — everything portable, brokered fairly, prices high but quality vouched-for; the place to buy a rare item the seller cannot place locally
- **Vorok holdings** — heavy plate, mining tools, contract-bound enchantments rare elsewhere

Generate shop inventories matching the city. Avenor doesn't sell Brotherhood staves openly; Telinor doesn't sell phial-bandoliers; Hofnar wagons sell anything they can move.

### Black Market and Witch Commissions

Hedge-witches and back-alley apothecaries broker witch-enchanted gear, illegal mana phials, captive-source charms, items above what the city legally licenses. Higher tier than what's openly available, but legally hot to carry. Crossing a kingdom border with a Brotherhood-stamped suppression tool, a black-market mana phial, or an enchanted item without a writ is a problem if a checkpoint catches it.

## When Generating Items in Play

### In Shops

Match city specialty (above). Tier matches local power level — a small Whitestone fishing town carries Common gear with the occasional Uncommon piece; a major Avenorian markethouse stocks up to Rare openly and Epic with introduction. Don't seed Legendary in shops; those are unique finds.

### As Loot from a Defeated Foe

Tier scales with foe difficulty. A Tier 1 creature (auroch, husk) drops Common kit at most. Tier 3 foes (named bandits, soul-war remnants) can carry Rare. Epic loot drops only from named, narratively-significant foes. Legendary loot is reserved for quest payoffs — earned, not random.

### From a Ruin or Vault

Pre-Ensoulment ruins are the home of Legendary uniques. Seed sparingly: one named Legendary item per major ruin clear, plus mundane Common-Rare scattered as period pieces. Items pulled from a vault need apothecary identification — describe them by appearance only until identified, then reveal name, history, and bonus structure on a successful alchemy/investigation check or a paid identification.

### As Quest Reward

Quest rewards are narratively framed: the item is *granted*, not picked up off a corpse. Tier matches the quest's significance. A side-quest reward is Uncommon or Rare; a major-arc reward is Epic or Legendary, often a unique commission or a recovered relic.

## Currency

The world's currency is **gold, silver, copper** (1 gold = 10 silver = 100 copper). See `generateStory.custom` for the canonical price benchmarks. Rough gear-tier guidance:

- Common gear: 2 silver to 2 gold (a smith-issue dagger, a workman's tunic)
- Uncommon gear: 2–8 gold (proper armor, a journeyman's blade, College-issue reagent kit)
- Rare gear: 10–30 gold (a hand-fitted master's piece, a senior witch's bandolier)
- Epic gear: 50–200 gold (chapterhouse-grade, master's instrument, only stocked in major cities and only with introduction)
- Legendary gear: rarely sold openly; if sold, several hundred to thousands of gold, and the seller is doing something complicated
- Common consumable (healing potion, basic mana potion): 5–15 silver
- Master-grade mana potion (alchemically distilled, licensed Avenorian house): 1–3 gold
- Identification of an unknown pre-Ensoulment item by a senior apothecary: 5 silver to 5 gold depending on quality of work, plus a percentage of the eventual sale

Players begin with starting coin appropriate to their trait. Wages: a competent mercenary earns 2–5 silver per day in the field, ~1 silver in garrison. A senior wizard or witch on contract earns far more, but their work is rationed.

## Consumables

Not tier-bracketed; described directly. Common types:

- **Healing potion** — alchemical, restores health (+30 to +40 per dose)
- **Bandages** — slower healing (+20)
- **Mana potion** — restores witch reservoir (+30 to +60 per dose for ordinary alchemy; up to +100+ for master-grade preparations from licensed Avenorian houses)
- **Reagent** — alchemical ingredient; not consumed alone, used to brew preparations or potentiate witch-workings
- **Black-source mana potion** — illegal preparation distilled from non-consensual essence; +60 to +120, but possession is a kingdom-crime nearly everywhere

Consumables can be bought (apothecaries, witches, alchemy stalls), brewed (a character with the alchemy skill and reagents), or looted. The alchemy skill is the non-mage gateway to magical preparations.

## Pre-Ensoulment Uniques

Legendary items are the dopamine tier. Each one:

- Has a proper name in a forgotten tongue or a historic title (the Staff of the First Spring, Ashbringer, the Ironheart Veil)
- Has a story in its description — who made it, what it was for, who carried it last, how it was lost
- Is referenced in world-lore where appropriate, so a learned NPC can recognize it on sight
- Cannot be replicated in the present age — the methods are lost; the closest a modern witch can get is paying hundreds of mana and days of focus to lay a single working

Do not generate generic Legendary items. Each one is hand-authored.

## Don't Generate

- A wizard staff held by a non-wizard NPC unless the narrative explicitly establishes how (stolen, scavenged, gift from a wizard who owes a debt) — and even then it does not work for them
- A witch phial-bandolier on a fighter unless the same narrative justification exists
- An item whose tier exceeds the local power level without specific narrative reason (a Legendary blade in a backwater shop is wrong)
- Multiple Legendary items in a single encounter (the Legendary tier is rare; oversaturating it kills the dopamine)
- An item with bonus magnitudes outside the rarity scaling above without explicit justification
- A pre-Ensoulment relic that has been identified without an apothecary or alchemist involved at some point in its history
